explanation: Step Functions provides native Retry/Catch error handling in its state machine definition (ASL), enabling conditional retries and fallback logic across Lambda, SageMaker, and Glue steps.

question: An ML engineer wants to deploy CPU-based inference models for 40 different customer segments while minimizing infrastructure cost. Which SageMaker deployment option should the engineer use?

options.A: Deploy 40 separate real-time endpoints.
options.B: Deploy 40 separate serverless endpoints.
options.C: Deploy a single multi-model endpoint (MME) hosting all 40 models.
options.D: Use batch transform for each customer segment.

explanation: Multi-model endpoints (MME) host multiple models on shared instances, dynamically loading from S3 on demand — the most cost-efficient option for serving many infrequently-used models.

question: An ML engineer needs pre-processing, model inference, and post-processing to run in a single inference request pipeline on a SageMaker endpoint. Which feature should the engineer use?

options.A: Multi-container endpoint with SageMaker Inference Pipeline.
options.B: Multi-model endpoint.
options.C: Separate endpoints chained via Amazon API Gateway.
options.D: Batch transform with custom preprocessing code.

explanation: SageMaker Inference Pipeline chains 2–15 containers sequentially in one endpoint, enabling pre-processing → model → post-processing in a single inference request.

question: A company wants to automatically retrain a model when SageMaker Model Monitor detects data drift. Which architecture implements this event-driven retraining pattern?

options.A: SageMaker Model Monitor → Amazon CloudWatch alarm → Amazon EventBridge → SageMaker Pipelines execution.
options.B: A cron job running on an Amazon EC2 instance that checks model metrics daily.
options.C: Manual retraining after a data scientist reviews Model Monitor dashboards.
options.D: AWS Glue trigger based on new S3 data arrivals.

explanation: Model Monitor → CloudWatch alarm → EventBridge → Pipelines/Step Functions is the canonical event-driven retraining architecture for automated drift response on AWS.

question: A company needs to run an on-demand bias-drift analysis for a model deployed to a SageMaker real-time endpoint. Which approach meets this requirement?

options.A: Configure the application to invoke an AWS Lambda function that runs a SageMaker Clarify processing job.
options.B: Use a Lambda function to pull the sagemaker-model-monitor-analyzer container image.
options.C: Use AWS Glue Data Quality checks.
options.D: Manually compare predictions in SageMaker notebooks.

explanation: SageMaker Clarify processing jobs run bias/explainability analysis on demand. Lambda provides serverless invocation, making it easy to trigger Clarify checks programmatically.

question: A fintech company's fraud detection model deployed on a SageMaker endpoint requires per-inference feature attributions (SHAP values) without changing the model code. Which SageMaker feature provides this capability?

options.A: SageMaker Model Monitor
options.B: SageMaker Clarify online explainer configured on the deployed endpoint.
options.C: Amazon CloudWatch A/B distribution analysis.
options.D: SageMaker shadow endpoints.

explanation: SageMaker Clarify's online explainer adds real-time SHAP feature attributions to endpoint responses via endpoint configuration — no model code changes needed.

question: A SageMaker serverless inference endpoint shows higher latency in production than during testing. The ML engineer suspects the issue is related to model startup time (cold starts). Which CloudWatch metric should the engineer monitor?

options.A: Schedule SageMaker Model Monitor for model-quality metrics.
options.B: Use Model Monitor with custom CloudWatch metrics.
options.C: Observe the ModelSetupTime CloudWatch metric in the AWS/SageMaker namespace.
options.D: Observe the ModelLoadingWaitTime metric.

explanation: ModelSetupTime in the AWS/SageMaker CloudWatch namespace directly measures the cold-start duration for serverless endpoints — the time to provision compute and load the model.

question: A data science team's SageMaker notebooks must access training data in Amazon S3, but no internet access is allowed, and all data traffic must stay on the AWS network. Which configuration meets these requirements?

options.A: Place the notebook in a private subnet. Put the SageMaker endpoint and S3 bucket in the same VPC.
options.B: Place the notebook in a private subnet with IAM policies only.
options.C: Place the notebook in a private subnet with an S3 VPC gateway endpoint and a SageMaker interface endpoint (AWS PrivateLink).
options.D: Place the notebook in a private subnet with a NAT gateway for S3 and SageMaker access.

explanation: S3 VPC gateway endpoints + SageMaker interface endpoints (PrivateLink) route all traffic through the AWS backbone, eliminating internet exposure while enabling full S3 and SageMaker API access.

question: A financial services company wants to control data egress from Amazon SageMaker. Which THREE mechanisms directly prevent data from leaving the AWS network? (Choose three.)

options.A: VPC interface endpoint (AWS PrivateLink) for SageMaker.
options.B: AWS Organizations Service Control Policies (SCPs) to restrict access.
options.C: Disable root access on SageMaker notebook instances.
options.D: Enable network isolation for training jobs and models.
options.E: Restrict notebook presigned URLs to specific IP addresses.

explanation: PrivateLink keeps SageMaker API traffic on AWS backbone; network isolation blocks all outbound from training containers; IP-restricted presigned URLs prevent off-network notebook access.

question: A distributed training job runs on 6 ml.p3.8xlarge instances in private subnets. The ML engineer must encrypt ALL inter-node traffic during training without changing at-rest encryption settings. Which action meets this requirement?

options.A: Enable encrypted communications for SageMaker Batch Transform.
options.B: Enable inter-container traffic encryption for the SageMaker training job.
options.C: Specify a KMS key in the training job request for volume encryption.
options.D: Specify a KMS key when creating the SageMaker Studio domain.

explanation: Inter-container traffic encryption (EnableInterContainerTrafficEncryption=true) adds TLS encryption to all node-to-node communication during distributed training jobs.

question: A healthcare startup must proactively prevent SageMaker notebook instances from ever being created with root access across 8 AWS accounts, regardless of whether provisioning is done via Console, CLI, Service Catalog, or CloudFormation. Which approach is MOST effective?

options.A: Apply IAM condition key sagemaker:RootAccess=Disabled on CreateNotebookInstance and UpdateNotebookInstance, enforced via SCPs across all accounts.
options.B: Encrypt EBS volumes attached to notebook instances with AWS KMS.
options.C: Use Amazon EventBridge with AWS Lambda to delete non-compliant notebooks within 60 seconds.
options.D: Use AWS CloudFormation stack-event triggers with Lambda to delete non-compliant stacks.

explanation: IAM condition key sagemaker:RootAccess=Disabled on CreateNotebookInstance, enforced via SCPs across all accounts, proactively blocks root-access notebooks at the API authorization layer.

question: An education platform has 5 TB of labeled data in one Amazon S3 bucket with per-department prefixes. 50 ML engineers must only read and write data from their own department's prefix via SageMaker Studio. Which approach ensures least-privilege access?

options.A: Enable S3 versioning for each department prefix.
options.B: Configure S3 Object Lock per user.
options.C: Add CORS rules to the S3 bucket.
options.D: Create IAM policies scoped to department-specific S3 prefixes and attach them to SageMaker execution roles.

explanation: IAM policies with S3 prefix conditions (s3:prefix) on execution roles restrict each ML engineer to their department's S3 prefix — enforcing least-privilege data access.

question: A SageMaker notebook must read a training dataset stored in Amazon S3 that is encrypted with SSE-KMS. Which configuration allows the notebook to access this data?

options.A: Configure security groups to allow HTTPS traffic to S3.
options.B: Configure VPC access and add the notebook's KMS role to the KMS key policy.
options.C: Assign an IAM role to the notebook with S3 read access, and grant that role decrypt permission in the KMS key policy.
options.D: Assign the S3 KMS key directly to the notebook instance configuration.

explanation: The notebook's execution role needs both s3:GetObject on the bucket AND kms:Decrypt permission granted in the KMS key policy — both are required to read SSE-KMS encrypted objects.

question: A company wants to detect and quantify bias in training data and model predictions across protected groups (e.g., ensuring demographic parity < 0.1). Which AWS service provides this capability?

options.A: Amazon CloudWatch network and CPU monitoring.
options.B: AWS Glue DataBrew recipes based on output statistics.
options.C: Amazon SageMaker Clarify for pre-training and post-training bias and explainability analysis.
options.D: AWS Lambda input-quality enforcement.

explanation: SageMaker Clarify computes statistical fairness metrics (demographic parity, disparate impact, equalized odds) on both training data and model predictions for bias detection.

question: An XGBoost subscription-churn model's F1 score drops significantly months after deployment. SageMaker Model Monitor has flagged the deviation. What is the MOST likely cause?

options.A: Concept drift — the relationship between features and target has changed.
options.B: The model was underfitting from the beginning.
options.C: The baseline data used for Model Monitor had quality issues.
options.D: Incorrect ground truth labels were used during baseline creation.

explanation: Concept drift — the real-world relationship between features and churn behavior has changed over time, making the original model's learned patterns no longer accurate.

question: After updating a production model, SageMaker Model Monitor detects data quality issues in the new production traffic. What should the ML engineer do?

options.A: Adjust the model's hyperparameters.
options.B: Manually run Model Monitor on the most recent batch of data.
options.C: Create a new baseline from the latest dataset and update Model Monitor to use it.
options.D: Add more training data and redeploy the original model.

explanation: After updating a model, the old baseline no longer reflects current data distributions. Creating a new baseline ensures Model Monitor compares against the correct reference distribution.

question: An ML engineer must track how often data scientists deploy models, monitor GPU/CPU utilization on endpoints, and detect invocation errors. Which TWO AWS services integrate with SageMaker for these monitoring needs? (Choose two.)

options.A: AWS CloudTrail
options.B: AWS Health Dashboard
options.C: AWS Trusted Advisor
options.D: Amazon CloudWatch

explanation: CloudTrail logs all SageMaker API calls (deployments, updates); CloudWatch captures operational metrics (CPU, GPU, invocation count, errors, latency) from SageMaker endpoints.

question: An ML team needs to process inference requests with payloads larger than 6 MB while also monitoring for data quality drift. Which combination of SageMaker features should the engineer use?

options.A: Amazon ECS Fargate with AWS CloudTrail alerts.
options.B: Amazon EKS with Amazon EventBridge alerts.
options.C: SageMaker Batch Transform with SageMaker Model Monitor.
options.D: SageMaker asynchronous endpoint with CloudWatch Logs Insights custom queries.

explanation: Batch Transform handles arbitrarily large datasets from S3 without payload size limits. Model Monitor can run on batch transform output to detect data quality drift.

question: An ML team deploys a model and wants to compare its predictions on live data with ground truth labels that arrive several days later. Which SageMaker Model Monitor type should they use?

options.A: Model Quality Monitor with merged ground-truth data.
options.B: Data Quality Monitor.
options.C: Bias Drift Monitor only.
options.D: Feature Attribution Drift Monitor only.

explanation: Model Quality Monitor merges delayed ground truth labels with captured predictions, computing accuracy/F1/RMSE trends over time to track ongoing model performance.

question: An ML team deploys a large language model and wants to detect significant changes in the distribution of feature importance (SHAP values) over time. Which SageMaker monitoring capability should they use?

options.A: Data Quality Monitor
options.B: Model Quality Monitor
options.C: Feature Attribution Drift Monitor (SageMaker Clarify via Model Monitor)
options.D: Bias Drift Monitor

explanation: Feature Attribution Drift Monitor (Clarify via Model Monitor) tracks SHAP value distributions over time, alerting when feature importance rankings shift significantly from baseline.

question: A company needs to automatically alert the ML team and trigger model retraining when SageMaker Model Monitor reports a constraint violation. Which architecture implements this pattern?

options.A: SageMaker Model Monitor → Amazon CloudWatch alarm → Amazon SNS/EventBridge → AWS Step Functions or SageMaker Pipelines retraining.
options.B: Amazon SNS alert only with manual retraining.
options.C: Amazon QuickSight dashboard review by data scientists.
options.D: AWS CloudTrail log analysis with periodic manual checks.

explanation: Model Monitor publishes constraint violation metrics to CloudWatch → alarms trigger EventBridge rules → EventBridge invokes Pipelines/Step Functions for automated retraining.

question: An ML engineer wants to encrypt ML storage volumes attached to SageMaker training jobs, endpoints, and Processing jobs using a customer-managed encryption key. How should the engineer configure this?

options.A: Pass an AWS KMS customer-managed key (CMK) ARN in the job configuration (VolumeKmsKeyId or KmsKeyId).
options.B: Use Amazon S3 SSE-S3 encryption for all storage volumes.
options.C: Use client-side PGP encryption before uploading data.
options.D: AWS KMS cannot be used with SageMaker storage volumes.

explanation: SageMaker accepts customer-managed KMS CMK ARNs (VolumeKmsKeyId) for encrypting EBS storage volumes on training instances, endpoints, and Processing jobs with customer-controlled keys.

question: An ML engineer must prevent training-serving skew by ensuring the same feature transformations are used for both offline training and online inference. Which AWS service helps achieve this by design?

options.A: Amazon SageMaker Feature Store (with online and offline stores).
options.B: Two independent AWS Glue ETL jobs — one for training and one for inference.
options.C: Duplicate transformation code in the inference application.
options.D: Use Amazon Athena to query raw data at inference time.

explanation: SageMaker Feature Store's shared online/offline stores use identical feature definitions and transformations, preventing the feature engineering inconsistencies that cause training-serving skew.

question: A company ingests real-time ecommerce transactions and needs sub-second anomaly detection using a SageMaker model. Which architecture meets this latency requirement?

options.A: Amazon S3 → AWS Glue ETL → SageMaker Batch Transform.
options.B: Amazon Kinesis Data Streams → AWS Lambda → SageMaker real-time endpoint.
options.C: Amazon MSK on EC2 → AWS Lambda → SageMaker endpoint.
options.D: Amazon SQS FIFO → AWS Lambda → AWS Glue ETL (batch).

explanation: Kinesis Data Streams → Lambda → SageMaker real-time endpoint is the canonical AWS architecture for sub-second streaming inference on individual events.

question: A company ingests data via Amazon Data Firehose (60-second buffer) into Amazon OpenSearch Service but needs sub-second delivery latency. Which configuration change addresses this requirement?

options.A: Enable zero buffering in Firehose and tune the PutRecordBatch size.
options.B: Replace Firehose with AWS DataSync and enable enhanced fan-out.
options.C: Increase the Firehose buffer to 120 seconds for larger batches.
options.D: Replace Firehose with Amazon SQS for message queuing.

explanation: Firehose zero buffering eliminates the default 60-second batching delay, enabling near-instant delivery. Tuning PutRecordBatch size optimizes throughput for sub-second use cases.

question: An ML engineer needs to explore a very large hyperparameter space and wants to minimize both cost and wall-clock time. Which approach is MOST effective?

options.A: Grid search on a single large instance.
options.B: Manual tuning in Jupyter notebooks.
options.C: SageMaker Automatic Model Tuning with parallel jobs, Bayesian optimization strategy, and Spot instances.
options.D: Random search using CLI scripts on an EC2 instance.

explanation: SageMaker AMT with Bayesian optimization efficiently navigates large hyperparameter spaces. Running parallel tuning jobs on Spot instances minimizes both wall-clock time and cost.
